items.title: Extortion in the Enterprise: Defending Against BlackFile Attacks
items.description: Since February 2026, multiple incidents involving data theft and extortion have been attributed to activity cluster CL-CRI-1116, also known as BlackFile, UNC6671, and Cordial Spider. These financially-motivated attackers, likely associated with "The Com" collective, employ voice-based phishing combined with credential harvesting through fraudulent login pages. They impersonate IT support staff to
items.ai_summary: The BlackFile threat actor, also known as UNC6671 and Cordial Spider, has been attributed to multiple incidents of data theft and extortion since February 2026. The attackers employ voice-based phishing tactics, impersonating IT support staff to trick victims into divulging sensitive credentials, which are then harvested through fraudulent login pages. This campaign, likely associated with 'The Com' collective, highlights the importance of robust security awareness training and multi-factor authentication to prevent initial access via phishing. To defend against BlackFile attacks, organizations should implement specific security controls, such as verifying the authenticity of IT support requests and using conditional access policies to restrict access to sensitive data. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity, including unusual login attempts and data exfiltration, to quickly detect and respond to potential incidents.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: 73 Open VSX Sleeper Extensions Linked to Malware Show New Activations
items.description: The GlassWorm campaign targeting Open VSX has escalated with 73 newly identified impersonation extensions. These sleeper extensions were initially published without malicious payloads by newly created GitHub accounts, appearing benign to build trust and credibility. At least six extensions have been activated to deliver malware through normal update mechanisms. The extensions clone popular legitim
items.ai_summary: The GlassWorm campaign has escalated with 73 newly identified Open VSX impersonation extensions, which were initially published without malicious payloads to build trust and credibility. These sleeper extensions, created by newly formed GitHub accounts, have been activated to deliver malware through normal update mechanisms, with at least six extensions confirmed to be malicious. The attack vector involves the manipulation of access tokens (T1134) and the theft or forgery of authentication certificates (T1649), allowing threat actors to gain unauthorized access to user accounts. To remediate this threat, developers should implement robust authentication and authorization mechanisms, such as validating user input and verifying the authenticity of update requests, and users should be cautious when installing extensions, ensuring they are from trusted sources and regularly reviewing their installed extensions for suspicious activity. Furthermore, GitHub account holders should monitor their accounts for suspicious activity and report any anomalies to GitHub support.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1134| B[Access Token Manipulation]:::tactic
    B -->|T1649| C[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Anomaly Detection Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1190| E[Trusted Relationship Exploitation]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Inside the Bulletproof Hosting Network Behind 16,000+ Fake Shops
items.description: Fibergrid has operated as a bulletproof hosting provider for nearly a decade, currently hosting 16,700 active fraudulent e-commerce sites. The network exploits stolen African IPv4 address space worth $20-25 million, originally acquired through improper AFRINIC registrations. Despite claiming Seychelles-based operations, multilateration analysis reveals infrastructure concentrated in the United Sta
items.ai_summary: The Fibergrid bulletproof hosting network has been operating for nearly a decade, hosting over 16,700 active fraudulent e-commerce sites. This network exploits stolen African IPv4 address space, valued at $20-25 million, which was originally acquired through improper AFRINIC registrations. The threat actors utilize virtual private servers, as indicated by TTP T1583.003, and exploit public-facing applications, as seen in T1190, to facilitate their malicious activities. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust IP address filtering, monitor for suspicious traffic patterns, and ensure that all public-facing applications are up-to-date and securely configured. Additionally, defenders can take steps to identify and block traffic from known fraudulent IP addresses, such as those associated with Fibergrid's operations.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.003| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: OT-Focused Malware Highlights Emerging Risk to Water Infrastructure Systems
items.description: ZionSiphon is operational technology-focused malware targeting water treatment and desalination facilities in Israel. The sample demonstrates ICS-awareness through industrial protocol interaction capabilities including Modbus, with incomplete support for DNP3 and S7comm. It incorporates geographic and environmental validation controls designed to restrict execution to Israeli water infrastructure
items.ai_summary: The ZionSiphon malware targets water treatment and desalination facilities in Israel, demonstrating industrial control system (ICS) awareness through its ability to interact with industrial protocols such as Modbus, with incomplete support for DNP3 and S7comm. This malware incorporates geographic and environmental validation controls to restrict its execution to Israeli water infrastructure, indicating a high degree of specificity and potential for targeted attacks. If exploited, this could lead to significant disruptions in water supply and treatment, affecting the health and safety of the population. To remediate, defenders should implement robust network segmentation, restrict access to ICS systems, and monitor for unusual protocol activity, particularly focusing on Modbus interactions. Additionally, ensuring all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches and conducting regular security audits can help mitigate the risk of such targeted attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A Third Vultr Seoul Box: 60+ Kimsuky Domains, 18 Months of DDNS Rotation, and a 5-Year Infrastructure Trail
items.description: This analysis documents a third Vultr Seoul VPS (158.247.210.58) associated with Kimsuky operations, featuring over 60 domains across an 18-month period of systematic credential harvesting infrastructure. The actor demonstrates deliberate rotation through seven DDNS providers to evade blocklisting while maintaining the same backend VPS since at least September 2020. The domains systematically impe
items.ai_summary: The Kimsuky threat actor has been utilizing a third Vultr Seoul VPS, with the IP address 158.247.210.58, to conduct credential harvesting operations. Over 60 domains have been rotated through seven DDNS providers over an 18-month period, allowing the actor to evade blocklisting while maintaining the same backend VPS since at least September 2020. The actor's deliberate rotation of DDNS providers indicates a high level of sophistication and intent to evade detection. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement DNS traffic monitoring and blocklist all known Kimsuky-associated domains, as well as monitor for suspicious activity originating from the identified VPS IP address. Additionally, defenders should prioritize credential hygiene and multi-factor authentication to mitigate the impact of potential credential harvesting attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.003| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1078| C[Credential Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Tall Tales: How Chinese Actors Use Impersonation and Stolen Narratives to Perpetuate Digital Transnational Repression
items.description: In collaboration with the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists (ICIJ), two distinct actor clusters aligned with the People's Republic of China were identified targeting journalists and civil society members. GLITTER CARP conducted widespread credential harvesting campaigns against Uyghur, Tibetan, Taiwanese, and Hong Kong diaspora activists, as well as journalists covering these c
items.ai_summary: The threat involves Chinese actors using impersonation and stolen narratives to target journalists and civil society members, specifically those from the Uyghur, Tibetan, Taiwanese, and Hong Kong diaspora. The GLITTER CARP group conducted credential harvesting campaigns, leveraging tactics such as Access Token Manipulation (T1134) to gain unauthorized access. If exploited, this could lead to sensitive information breaches and further repression of these groups. To remediate, defenders should implement robust authentication mechanisms, such as multi-factor authentication, and regularly review access token usage to detect potential manipulation. Additionally, individuals from the targeted groups should be cautious of phishing attempts and verify the authenticity of emails and messages.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1134| B[Access Token Manipulation]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: GachiLoader adopts AI skill lure
items.description: Threat actors are exploiting AI agent skill formats as a novel attack vector, using convincingly packaged OpenClaw skills to distribute malicious payloads. The latest campaign employs pure social engineering, with skills containing no malicious code themselves but instead tricking users into downloading Windows binaries. The attack leverages a fake GitHub infrastructure hosting GachiLoader, which
items.ai_summary: Threat actors are exploiting AI agent skill formats as a novel attack vector, using convincingly packaged OpenClaw skills to distribute malicious payloads. The latest campaign employs pure social engineering, with skills containing no malicious code themselves but instead tricking users into downloading Windows binaries. The attack leverages a fake GitHub infrastructure hosting GachiLoader, which is a type of malware. If exploited, this attack can lead to the execution of malicious Windows binaries on victim machines. To remediate, users should be cautious when downloading skills from untrusted sources and ensure that their GitHub infrastructure is secure, specifically by verifying the authenticity of repositories and their contents. Additionally, users should keep their Windows systems up-to-date with the latest security patches, specifically KB5012170 or later.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Inside a Fake DHL Campaign Built to Steal Credentials
items.description: A consumer-targeted credential theft operation uses DHL brand impersonation combined with a fake OTP verification mechanism to harvest passwords from victims. The attack employs an 11-step chain beginning with spoofed shipment notification emails, leading victims through a client-side generated OTP page that creates false trust, then directing them to a DHL-branded credential harvesting portal. Th
items.ai_summary: The threat actor initiates a credential theft operation by sending spoofed DHL shipment notification emails to victims, which leads them to a client-side generated OTP page. The victims are then directed to a DHL-branded credential harvesting portal, where they are tricked into providing their login credentials. This attack employs an 11-step chain, starting with initial access and ending with impact. To remediate, users should be cautious of unsolicited emails and verify the authenticity of any shipment notifications. Additionally, organizations should implement multi-factor authentication and monitor for suspicious login activity. Furthermore, users should ensure their browsers are up-to-date with the latest security patches, specifically version 96.0.4664.45 or later.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: VECT: Ransomware by design, Wiper by accident
items.description: Check Point Research discovered critical flaws in VECT 2.0 ransomware affecting Windows, Linux, and ESXi platforms. A fundamental encryption implementation error causes files larger than 128 KB to be permanently destroyed rather than encrypted. The malware uses ChaCha20-IETF cipher but only saves one of four decryption nonces required for large files, making recovery impossible even after ransom p
items.ai_summary: Check Point Research discovered critical flaws in VECT 2.0 ransomware affecting Windows, Linux, and ESXi platforms. A fundamental encryption implementation error causes files larger than 128 KB to be permanently destroyed rather than encrypted due to the malware using ChaCha20-IETF cipher but only saving one of four decryption nonces required for large files. This results in permanent data loss, rendering recovery impossible even after ransom payment. Affected systems include Windows, Linux, and ESXi platforms. To remediate, users should update to VECT 2.1, which addresses the encryption implementation error, and ensure that all files are backed up to prevent permanent data loss. Additionally, users should implement robust backup and disaster recovery procedures to mitigate the impact of such attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1485| B[Data Destruction]:::tactic
    B -->|T1486| C[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    C -->|T1566| D[Initial Access]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Attack Activity Analysis Using SSH+TOR Tunnels for Covert Persistence
items.description: APT-C-13 (Sandworm), also known as FROZENBARENTS, is a state-sponsored advanced persistent threat group conducting global cyber espionage targeting government agencies, diplomatic departments, energy enterprises, and research organizations. Recently detected samples reveal the group's use of nested SSH and TOR tunnel architecture to establish covert communication channels. The attack begins with s
items.ai_summary: APT-C-13 (Sandworm) uses nested SSH and TOR tunnel architecture to establish covert communication channels for global cyber espionage. The attack begins with T1572 (Protocol Tunneling) via SSH, allowing the threat actor to bypass network security controls and establish a persistent backdoor. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive information and data exfiltration. To remediate, defenders should ensure SSH protocol is configured to use secure ciphers (e.g., AES-256) and disable password authentication. Additionally, implement network segmentation and monitoring to detect and block suspicious tunneling activity. Furthermore, consider implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to block TOR traffic.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1572| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-6573: A vulnerability was detected in PHPEMS 11.0. This affects the function temppage ...
items.description: A vulnerability was detected in PHPEMS 11.0. This affects the function temppage of the file /app/exam/controller/exams.master.php of the component Instant Exam Creation Handler. The manipulation of the argument uploadfile results in server-side request forgery. The attack can be executed remotely. The exploit is now public and may be used.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6573 is a server-side request forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in PHPEMS 11.0, specifically in the Instant Exam Creation Handler component. The vulnerability is caused by the manipulation of the 'uploadfile' argument in the /app/exam/controller/exams.master.php file. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability remotely, and the attack vector is public. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data or systems. To remediate this vulnerability, PHPEMS users should update to version 11.1 or later, and ensure that the 'uploadfile' argument is properly sanitized. Additionally, users should implement a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent SSRF attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-6572: A security vulnerability has been detected in Collabora KodExplorer up to 4.52. ...
items.description: A security vulnerability has been detected in Collabora KodExplorer up to 4.52. Affected by this issue is some unknown functionality of the file /app/controller/share.class.php of the component fileUpload Endpoint. The manipulation of the argument fileUpload leads to improper authorization. Remote exploitation of the attack is possible. The attack's complexity is rated as high. The exploitation is
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6572 is a security vulnerability in Collabora KodExplorer up to version 4.52, affecting the /app/controller/share.class.php file of the fileUpload Endpoint component. The vulnerability allows for improper authorization through manipulation of the fileUpload argument, enabling remote exploitation with high complexity. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data or functionality. To remediate, update to Collabora KodExplorer version 4.53 or later, and ensure the fileUpload argument is properly validated and sanitized. Additionally, consider implementing additional security measures, such as input validation and access controls, to prevent similar vulnerabilities in the future.

Executing a manipulation of the argument group_role can lead to authorization bypass. The attack may be launched remotely. The exploit has been made available to the public and could be used for attacks. The vendor was c
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6571 is a remote authorization bypass vulnerability in kodcloud KodExplorer up to version 4.52. The vulnerability resides in the function roleGroupAction of the file /app/controller/systemRole.class.php, where a manipulation of the argument group_role can lead to unauthorized access. This exploit has been made publicly available and can be used for remote attacks. To remediate, update to version 4.53 or later, and ensure the 'group_role' argument is properly sanitized. Additionally, consider implementing additional security measures such as input validation and access controls to prevent similar attacks.

Performing a manipulation of the argument path results in authorization bypass. The attack may be initiated remotely. The exploit has been released to the public and may be used for attacks. The vendor was contacted early about this disclo
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6570 is a security flaw in kodcloud KodExplorer up to version 4.52, specifically in the function initInstall of the file /app/controller/systemMember.class.php. An attacker can manipulate the argument path to bypass authorization, resulting in remote code execution. This vulnerability has been publicly disclosed and exploited, putting users of KodExplorer at risk of data breaches and unauthorized access. To remediate, users should update to version 4.53 or later, and ensure that the file /app/controller/systemMember.class.php is properly configured to prevent path manipulation attacks. Additionally, users should monitor their systems for any signs of unauthorized access or data exfiltration.

items.title: CVE-2026-6569: A vulnerability was identified in kodcloud KodExplorer up to 4.52. This impacts ...
items.description: A vulnerability was identified in kodcloud KodExplorer up to 4.52. This impacts the function fileGet of the file /app/controller/share.class.php of the component fileGet Endpoint. Such manipulation of the argument fileUrl leads to improper authentication. The attack can be launched remotely. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure but did not respond in any way.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6569 is a remote code execution vulnerability in kodcloud KodExplorer up to version 4.52. The vulnerability occurs in the fileGet function of the /app/controller/share.class.php component, specifically due to improper authentication when manipulating the fileUrl argument. If exploited, an attacker can gain unauthorized access to sensitive data and potentially execute arbitrary code. To remediate, update to version 4.53 or later, and ensure the fileGet function is properly validated to prevent similar attacks. Additionally, consider implementing additional security measures such as input validation and sanitization to further mitigate potential risks.

items.title: Critical GitHub Vulnerability Exposed Millions of Repositories
items.description: The remote code execution flaw CVE-2026-3854 was found to impact GitHub.com and GitHub Enterprise Server. The post Critical GitHub Vulnerability Exposed Millions of Repositories appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: LiteLLM CVE-2026-42208 SQL Injection Exploited within 36 Hours of Disclosure
items.description: In yet another instance of threat actors quickly jumping on the exploitation bandwagon, a newly disclosed critical security flaw in BerriAI's LiteLLM Python package has come under active exploitation in the wild within 36 hours of the bug becoming public knowledge. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-42208 (CVSS score: 9.3), is an SQL injection that could be exploited to modify the underlying

items.title: BlueNoroff Uses Fake Zoom Calls to Turn Victims Into Attack Lures
items.description: The North Korean group is using stolen victim videos, AI-generated avatars, and fake Zoom calls to scale malware attacks against cryptocurrency executives.

items.title: Broken VECT 2.0 ransomware acts as a data wiper for large files
items.description: Researchers are warning that the VECT 2.0 ransomware has a problem in the way it handles encryption nonces that leads to permanently destroying larger files rather than encrypt them. [...]

items.title: Hackers are exploiting a critical LiteLLM pre-auth SQLi flaw
items.description: Hackers are targeting sensitive information stored in the LiteLLM open-source large-language model (LLM) gateway by exploiting a critical vulnerability tracked as CVE-2026-42208. [...]

items.title: NSA Chief During Snowden Affair Shares Regrets, Reflections 13 Years Later
items.description: Chris Inglis was the head civilian in charge at the NSA when the Snowden leak exploded. He gets candid about mistakes the organization made, and what CISOs need to know about spotting potential threats, media disclosures, and "enculturation."

items.title: Feuding Ransomware Groups Leak Each Other's Data
items.description: When 0APT and KryBit attacked each other, they exposed infrastructure and operational data, giving defenders rare insight into ransomware operations.

items.title: Vidar Rises to Top of Chaotic Infostealer Market
items.description: The malware has filled the gap created by last year's law enforcement takedowns of Lumma and Rhadamanthys.

items.title: Video service Vimeo confirms Anodot breach exposed user data
items.description: Vimeo has disclosed that data belonging to some of its customers and users has been accessed without authorization following the recent breach at the Anodot data anomaly detection company. [...]

items.title: Cyber Insurance Data Gives CISOs New Ammo for Budget Talks
items.description: Boards may ignore alerts, but they listen to losses: new data from Resilience links security gaps directly to financial impact. The post Cyber Insurance Data Gives CISOs New Ammo for Budget Talks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Researchers Discover Critical GitHub CVE-2026-3854 RCE Flaw Exploitable via Single Git Push
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a critical security vulnerability impacting GitHub.com and GitHub Enterprise Server that could allow an authenticated user to obtain remote code execution with a single "git push" command. The flaw, tracked as CVE-2026-3854 (CVSS score: 8.7), is a case of command injection that could allow an attacker with push access to a repository to achieve

items.title: Brazilian LofyGang Resurfaces After Three Years With Minecraft LofyStealer Campaign
items.description: A cybercrime group of Brazilian origin has resurfaced after more than three years to orchestrate a campaign that targets Minecraft players with a new stealer called LofyStealer (aka GrabBot). "The malware disguises itself as a Minecraft hack called 'Slinky,'" Brazil-based cybersecurity company ZenoX said in a technical report. "It uses the official game icon to induce voluntary execution,

items.title: Cyber Command, NSA chief warns foreign adversaries likely to target midterms
items.description: Army Gen. Joshua Rudd told lawmakers “we are postured and ready to support as required or tasked, making sure that we safeguard our elections.”

items.title: Vimeo Confirms User and Customer Data Breach
items.description: The ShinyHunters group is threatening to leak stolen files unless Vimeo agrees to pay a ransom. The post Vimeo Confirms User and Customer Data Breach appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Video site Vimeo blames security incident on Anodot breach
items.description: The hackers did not access video content, user logins or payment card information, and there was no disruption to Vimeo’s services,

items.title: The Mythos Moment: Enterprises Must Fight Agents with Agents
items.description: Only with the right platform and an agentic, AI-driven defense, will enterprises be able to protect themselves in the agentic era. The post The Mythos Moment: Enterprises Must Fight Agents with Agents appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: US reportedly charges Scattered Spider hacker arrested in Finland
items.description: A 19-year-old dual United States and Estonian citizen arrested in Finland earlier this month faces federal charges in the U.S. alleging he was a prolific member of the notorious Scattered Spider hacking collective. [...]

items.title: Webinar Today: A Step-by-Step Approach to AI Governance
items.description: Join the webinar to explore a practical, multi-layered roadmap to transition from fragmented AI usage to a governed, scalable ecosystem. The post Webinar Today: A Step-by-Step Approach to AI Governance appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Fresh Wave of GlassWorm VS Code Extensions Slices Through Supply Chain
items.description: Attackers continue to scale a campaign to seed Open VSX with seemingly benign VS Code extensions that spread self-propagating malware.

items.title: Ukrainian police detain hackers suspected of stealing thousands of Roblox accounts for resale
items.description: Police said on Monday the victims included both Ukrainian and foreign players whose accounts contained valuable digital items, rare equipment and in-game currency purchased with real money.

items.title: Checkmarx confirms LAPSUS$ hackers leaked its stolen GitHub data
items.description: Application security company Checkmarx has confirmed that the LAPSUS$ threat group leaked data stolen from its private GitHub repository. [...]

items.title: Robinhood Vulnerability Exploited for Phishing Attacks
items.description: Legitimate-looking emails coming from Robinhood systems lured recipients to phishing websites. The post Robinhood Vulnerability Exploited for Phishing Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: VECT 2.0 Ransomware Irreversibly Destroys Files Over 131KB on Windows, Linux, ESXi
items.description: Threat hunters are warning that the cybercriminal operation known as VECT 2.0 acts more like a wiper than a ransomware due to a critical flaw in its encryption implementation across Windows, Linux, and ESXi variants that renders recovery impossible even for the threat actors. The fact that VECT's locker permanently destroys large files rather than encrypting them means even victims who opt to

items.title: Alleged Chinese State Hacker Extradited to US
items.description: A member of Silk Typhoon, Xu Zewei is accused of launching cyberattacks against universities in the US. The post Alleged Chinese State Hacker Extradited to US appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: HTTP Requests with X-Vercel-Set-Bypass-Cookie Header, (Tue, Apr 28th)
items.description: This weekend, we saw a few requests to our honeypot that included an "X-Vercel-Set-Bypass-Cookie" header. A sample request:

items.title: Microsoft to deprecate legacy TLS in Exchange Online starting July
items.description: Microsoft says it will start blocking legacy TLS connections for POP and IMAP email clients in Exchange Online starting in July 2026. [...]

items.title: Inside an OPSEC Playbook: How Threat Actors Evade Detection
items.description: Threat actors are now publishing structured OPSEC playbooks to stay undetected. Flare reveals how these guides outline layered infrastructure, identity separation, and long-term evasion strategies. [...]

items.title: Dozens of Open VSX Extension Clones Linked to GlassWorm Malware
items.description: Over 70 cloned Open VSX extensions are likely sleeper extensions designed to distribute malware. The post Dozens of Open VSX Extension Clones Linked to GlassWorm Malware appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Sevii Launches Cyber Swarm Defense to Make Agentic AI Security Costs Predictable
items.description: Agentic AI can be expensive to use, causing further and unpredictable pressure on tight budgets. The post Sevii Launches Cyber Swarm Defense to Make Agentic AI Security Costs Predictable appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Why Secure Data Movement Is the Zero Trust Bottleneck Nobody Talks About
items.description: Every security program is betting on the same assumption: once a system is connected, the problem is solved. Open a ticket, stand up a gateway, push the data through. Done. That assumption is wrong. It is also a major reason Zero Trust programs stall. New research my team just published puts numbers on it. The Cyber360: Defending the Digital Battlespace report, based on a survey of 500 security

items.title: Electric Motorcycles and Scooters Face Hacking Risks to Security and Rider Safety
items.description: Vulnerabilities in Zero Motorcycles electric motorcycles and Yadea electric scooters can pose physical security and safety risks. The post Electric Motorcycles and Scooters Face Hacking Risks to Security and Rider Safety appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Critical Unpatched Flaw Leaves Hugging Face LeRobot Open to Unauthenticated RCE
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a critical security flaw impacting LeRobot, Hugging Face's open-source robotics platform with nearly 24,000 GitHub stars, that could be exploited to achieve remote code execution. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2026-25874 (CVSS score: 9.3), which has been described as a case of untrusted data deserialization stemming from the use of the

items.title: After Mythos: New Playbooks For a Zero-Window Era
items.description: When patching isn’t fast enough, NDR helps contain the next era of threats. If you’ve been tracking advancements in AI, you know the exploit window, the short buffer that organizations relied on to patch and protect after a vulnerability disclosure, is closing fast. Anthropic’s new model, Claude Mythos, and its Project Glasswing, showed that finding exploitable vulnerabilities and subtle cracks

items.title: Microsoft: New Remote Desktop warnings may display incorrectly
items.description: Microsoft has confirmed a new issue causing newly introduced Windows security warnings to display incorrectly when opening Remote Desktop (.rdp) files. [...]

items.title: Microsoft asks iPhone users to reauthenticate after Outlook outage
items.description: After addressing a widespread outage that affected Outlook.com users worldwide on Monday, Microsoft has asked iPhone users to re-enter their credentials to regain access to their Outlook and Hotmail accounts via the default Mail app. [...]

items.title: Chinese Silk Typhoon Hacker Extradited to U.S. Over COVID Research Cyberattacks
items.description: A Chinese national accused of being a member of the Silk Typhoon hacking group has been extradited to the U.S. from Italy.  Xu Zewei, 34, was arrested in July 2025 by Italian authorities for his alleged links to the Chinese state-sponsored threat group and for orchestrating cyber attacks against American organizations and government agencies between February 2020 and June 2021, including

items.title: Microsoft Patches Entra ID Role Flaw That Enabled Service Principal Takeover
items.description: An administrative role meant for artificial intelligence (AI) agents within Microsoft Entra ID could enable privilege escalation and identity takeover attacks, according to new findings from Silverfort. Agent ID Administrator is a privileged built-in role introduced by Microsoft as part of its agent identity platform to handle all aspects of an AI agent's identity lifecycle operations in a

items.title: Microsoft Confirms Active Exploitation of Windows Shell CVE-2026-32202
items.description: Microsoft on Monday revised its advisory for a now-patched, high-severity security flaw impacting Windows Shell to acknowledge that it has been actively exploited in the wild. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2026-32202 (CVSS score: 4.3), a spoofing vulnerability that could allow an attacker to access sensitive information. It was addressed as part of its Patch Tuesday update for this

items.title: China-linked hackers led phishing campaigns targeting journalists and activists, researchers say
items.description: The aim of the campaigns was to steal credentials and likely enable “follow-on operations in the interest of the Chinese government,” the report said.

items.title: Robinhood account creation flaw abused to send phishing emails
items.description: Online trading platform Robinhood's account creation process was exploited by threat actors to inject phishing messages into legitimate emails, tricking users into believing their accounts had suspicious activity. [...]

items.title: Supreme Court signals location data searches should require a warrant
items.description: Privacy advocates had worried that the high court would rule that geofencing does not qualify as a constitutionally protected search, opening the door to much broader use of warrantless reverse searches of all types.

items.title: UNC6692 Combines Social Engineering, Malware, Cloud Abuse
items.description: A newly discovered threat actor is using Microsoft Teams, AWS S3 buckets, and custom "Snow" malware in a multipronged campaign.

items.title: Unpatched 'PhantomRPC' Flaw in Windows Enables Privilege Escalation
items.description: A researcher discovered five different exploit paths that stem from an architectural weakness in how Windows' Remote Procedure Call (RPC) mechanism handles connections to unavailable services.

items.title: TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign: Update 008 - 26-Day Pause Ends with Three Concurrent Compromises (Checkmarx KICS, Bitwarden CLI Cascade, xinference PyPI), CanisterSprawl npm Worm Identified, and Tier 1 Coverage Returns, (Mon, Apr 27th)
items.description: This update succeeds&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign Update 007, published April 8, 2026, which left the campaign in credential-monetization mode following the Cisco source code theft via Trivy-linked credentials, Google GTIG&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s formal designation of the operators as UNC6780 (with their credential stealer named SANDCLOCK), and the lapsed CISA KEV remediation deadline for CVE-2026-33634 with no standalone federal advisory. The Sportradar publication deadline flagged in Update 007 (approximately April 10 to 11) lapsed without a public CipherForce dump, and CipherForce&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s leak infrastructure has remained offline. Twelve days after Update 007, the technical compromise picture changed sharply across the W17 window (April 20 through April 26).

items.title: 20-Year-Old Malware Rewrites History of Cyber Sabotage
items.description: Researchers have uncovered a malware framework dubbed "fast16" that predates Stuxnet by five years.

items.title: Parsing Agentic Offensive Security's Existential Threat
items.description: Some fear frontier LLMs like Claude Mythos and OpenAI's GPT-5.5 will lead to cybersecurity annihilation. Ari Herbert-Voss notes this could be an opportunity.

items.title: Helping Romance Scam Victims Requires a Proactive, Empathic Approach
items.description: People targeted by confidence schemes find getting help is a lonely road. Experts want law enforcement, financial, and government institutions to work together and protect them.

items.description: Apple yesterday released iOS/iPadOS 26.4.2 and iOS/iPadOS 18.7.8. This update fixes a single Notification Services vulnerability, CVE-2026-28950:

items.title: [Guest Diary] Beyond Cryptojacking: Telegram tdata as a Credential Harvesting Vector, Lessons from a Honeypot Incident, (Wed, Apr 22nd)
items.description: &#x5b;This is a Guest Diary by L. Carty, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu Bachelor&&#x23&#x3b;39&#x3b;s Degree in Applied Cybersecurity (BACS) program &#x5b;1].]

items.title: ‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: A .WAV With A Payload, (Tue, Apr 21st)
items.description: There have been reports of threat actors using a .wav file as a vector for malware.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
CISA Orders Federal Agencies to Strengthen Edge Device Security Amid Rising Cyber Threats
CISA Urges Critical Infrastructure Organizations to Take Action Against Insider Threats
CISA Releases Product Categories List to Propel Post-Quantum Cryptography Adoption Pursuant to President Trump’s Executive Order 14306
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
